nativeRange.habitat: Found on reef flats and in lagoon and seaward reef environments at depths of roughly 0 to 40 m. During the day the Five Lined Cardinalfish tends to shelter in dark crevices, under ledges, among branching corals, and notably among the long defensive spines of the sea urchin Diadema setosum, emerging at dusk to forage.

diet.notes: The Five Lined Cardinalfish is a carnivore that generally accepts a varied diet of meaty foods such as mysid shrimp, vitamin-enriched brine shrimp, finely chopped marine flesh, bloodworms, and quality marine pellets or flakes once settled. Evening or dusk feeding tends to improve acceptance, as this species naturally forages at night. Larger specimens have been reported to take small live feeder shrimp and bite-sized fish.

summary: The Five Lined Cardinalfish (Cheilodipterus quinquelineatus) is a moderately sized Indo-Pacific species reaching around 13 cm, recognized by five bold black stripes along a silvery body and a yellow spot at the tail base. Generally hardy and beginner-friendly, though it will prey on small shrimp and tiny fish.
careNotes:
The Five Lined Cardinalfish is one of the larger commonly imported cardinalfishes, distinguished by five sharp black horizontal stripes running along a silvery body and a small yellow spot at the base of the caudal fin. It is widely distributed across the Indo-Pacific from the Red Sea and East Africa east to the Pitcairn Group, and north to southern Japan, reaching a maximum size of around 13 cm. In the wild it occurs singly or in loose aggregations on reef flats and seaward reefs, often sheltering during the day in dark crevices, under ledges, among branching corals, or notably among the long defensive spines of the sea urchin Diadema setosum.

The species is largely nocturnal and tends to rest in shaded rockwork during the day, emerging at dusk to hunt small crustaceans, gastropods, and small fishes. In captivity this generally translates to a recommendation for plenty of caves, ledges, and shaded retreats, and evening feeding for newly added specimens.

The Five Lined Cardinalfish is generally safe with corals and sessile invertebrates, but should not be housed with small ornamental shrimp, tiny crabs, feather dusters (tube-dwelling worms with feathery crowns), or any fish small enough to be swallowed. A group of 3 to 5 individuals introduced together into a tank with ample rockwork tends to settle best, with conspecific aggression remaining low when space is adequate.
breedingNotes: The Five Lined Cardinalfish is a paternal mouthbrooder, a reproductive strategy common to its family (Apogonidae) in which the male incubates the eggs inside his mouth. The species shows distinct pair courtship and spawning; the male carries the egg mass in his buccal cavity for roughly 1 to 3 weeks and largely ceases feeding during brooding, with a visibly swollen jaw being the most obvious outward sign.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary husbandry source — gave scientific name (Cheilodipterus quinquelineatus), family (Apogonidae), care level (easy), temperament (semi-aggressive), minimum tank size (50 gal, 6-ft tank recommended for groups), reef-compatible (yes), diet (meaty carnivore), max size 5 in, water parameters 72-78°F / pH 8.1-8.4 / SG 1.020-1.025 / dKH 8-12, hides in seagrass / among urchin spines, slow methodical swimmer, hierarchy without aggression when space permits.

additional.name: Reef2Reef wiki
additional.notes: Designated secondary returned HTTP 403 Forbidden and could not be fetched; fell back to FishBase, Quality Marine, and Aquatic Community for cross-verification.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Taxonomy authority: Apogonidae, Kurtiformes; max size 13.0 cm TL; depth 0-40 m; Indo-Pacific distribution (Red Sea to Pitcairn Group, southern Japan to Lord Howe/Rapa); preferred wild temperature 24.9-29°C (mean 27.9); shelters in dark crevices, branched coral, ledges, and among Diadema setosum spines; nocturnal; diet of small crustaceans, gastropods, small fishes; mouthbrooder with distinct pair courtship; has been reared in captivity.

additional.name: Quality Marine
additional.notes: Cross-checked husbandry: 50 gal min, 72-78°F, pH 8.1-8.4, SG 1.020-1.025, dKH 8-12, ~5 in max, nocturnal (active at night, rests during day), schooling. Disagrees with LiveAquaria on reef-safety: explicitly warns NOT to house with smaller fish, shrimp, or feather dusters. Final written value 'with caution' reflects this spread. Notes 'some Cardinalfish are mouth brooders'.

additional.name: Aquatic Community
additional.notes: Confirmed max size 13.0 cm / 5.2 in, temperature 72-78°F, Indo-Pacific distribution, and nocturnal habit (hides during day, needs dark shelter). Notes the species feeds on small fishes, shrimp, and meaty foods, and that the species shouldn't be kept with larger predators. Breeding not documented in aquaria per this source.

fish.breedingNotes: Paternal mouthbrooder with distinct pair courtship; the male carries the egg mass in his buccal cavity for roughly 1 to 3 weeks and stops feeding during brooding. The species has been reared in captivity, though routine captive breeding remains uncommon in the hobby.
